metadata.title: Physics Informed Deep Learning (Part I): Data-driven Solutions of Nonlinear Partial Differential Equations

authors.name: Maziar Raissi
authors.affiliation: Division of Applied Mathematics, Brown University

authors.name: Paris Perdikaris
authors.affiliation: Department of Mechanical Engineering and Applied Mechanics, University of Pennsylvania

authors.name: George Em Karniadakis
authors.affiliation: Division of Applied Mathematics, Brown University

metadata.abstract_truncated: We introduce physics informed neural networks – neural networks trained to solve supervised learning tasks while respecting any given law of physics described by general nonlinear partial differential equations. We present data-driven solution and data-driven discovery of PDEs via continuous time and discrete time models.
key_claims: PINNs can solve nonlinear PDEs with as few as ~100 boundary/initial data points by encoding physics as a regularization term via collocation points.
Continuous-time PINNs encode PDE residuals at collocation points and train with L-BFGS via MSE over data + physics loss.
Discrete-time PINNs leverage implicit Runge-Kutta with arbitrarily many stages (up to 500), enabling single-step prediction across large time intervals.
Relative L2 errors are on the order of 1e-3 to 1e-4 across Burgers, Schrodinger, and Allen-Cahn equations.
The physics-informed loss term (MSEf) acts as a regularizer, preventing overfitting and enabling generalization from small datasets.

architecture.overview: Two classes of PINN models: (1) Continuous-time PINN and (2) Discrete-time PINN using implicit Runge-Kutta integration. Both use standard fully-connected feedforward neural networks (MLPs) with tanh activations and automatic differentiation to compute PDE residuals.

variants.description: Continuous-time PINN: a single MLP approximates u(t,x). A second 'physics network' f(t,x) is derived via autograd from u. Shared weights trained jointly.

modules.name: SolutionNetwork_u
modules.layers: Variable; benchmark default: 9 hidden layers x 20 neurons (Burgers), 5 hidden layers x 100 neurons (Schrodinger)
modules.notes: For Schrodinger: multi-output [u, v] where h = u + iv

modules.name: PhysicsResidualNetwork_f
modules.type: Derived via automatic differentiation from SolutionNetwork_u
modules.notes: No new parameters; shares all weights with SolutionNetwork_u

variants.description: Discrete-time PINN: A multi-output MLP predicts intermediate Runge-Kutta stage solutions and the final solution at t^{n+1} from inputs at t^n. Physics is embedded in the output layer via RK tableau weights.

modules.name: RK_SolutionNetwork
modules.output: [B, q+1]  where q = number of RK stages; outputs are [u^{n+c1}(x), ..., u^{n+cq}(x), u^{n+1}(x)]
modules.layers: 4 hidden layers x 50 neurons (Burgers discrete), 4 hidden layers x 200 neurons (Allen-Cahn)
modules.notes: Output dimension grows with q (500 stages => 501-dim output vector). Only last layer size scales with q.

modules.name: RK_PhysicsConstraint
modules.description: Computes u^n_i and u^n_{q+1} from RK tableau {a_ij, b_j, c_j} and network outputs via equations (9). Embedded in the loss.
modules.notes: Uses implicit Gauss-Legendre RK tableau (fully implicit, A-stable, arbitrary order)

ambiguities.primary_assumption: Xavier/Glorot uniform initialization (standard for tanh MLPs)

ambiguities.description: Specific Gauss-Legendre tableau coefficients not listed in paper; paper references Iserles [24]
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Standard q-stage Gauss-Legendre implicit RK collocation nodes and weights

pde_general_form.description: General parametrized nonlinear PDE. u(t,x) is the latent solution. N[·;λ] is a nonlinear differential operator.

physics_residual.role: Defines the physics residual network. Evaluated at collocation points. Enforced to be zero via MSEf.

MSEu.role: Data fidelity: penalizes deviation from initial/boundary conditions

MSEf.role: Physics regularization: penalizes PDE residual at collocation points

components.MSE0: Initial data loss on h(0, x)

SSEn.description: All q+1 stage predictions vs data at time t^n

components.SSEb: Boundary condition enforcement for all RK stages

equivalent_form.latex: u^n = u^n_i \text{ where } u^n_i := u^{n+c_i} + \Delta t \sum_{j=1}^q a_{ij} \mathcal{N}[u^{n+c_j}]
equivalent_form.role: Rewrite that allows the network to output stage solutions and compare against training data at t^n

tensors.name: t_u
tensors.role: input — time coords of boundary/IC data

tensors.name: x_u
tensors.role: input — spatial coords of boundary/IC data

tensors.name: u_data
tensors.role: target — known solution values at (t_u, x_u)

tensors.name: t_f
tensors.role: input — collocation time coords for physics loss

tensors.name: x_f
tensors.role: input — collocation spatial coords for physics loss

tensors.name: rk_stages_out
tensors.role: discrete PINN output — q intermediate RK stages + final solution

tensor_semantics.notes: B = batch size, Nu = number of IC/BC data points, Nf = number of collocation points, Nn = number of spatial data at snapshot t^n, q = number of RK stages

optimizer.name: L-BFGS
optimizer.notes: L-BFGS used for all benchmarks. Paper mentions Adam/SGD as alternatives for large datasets but does not use them in experiments.

alternative_optimizer.name: Adam / SGD
alternative_optimizer.notes: Mentioned for larger datasets; not used in benchmarks. If switching to Adam, use lr~1e-3.

training_pipeline.batch_size: Full batch (no mini-batching in paper experiments)
training_pipeline.num_epochs: Run until L-BFGS convergence (no fixed epoch count stated)

hardware.gpu: NVIDIA Titan X (training time ~60s for Burgers continuous)
hardware.notes: Target environment: single GPU. Paper uses TensorFlow 1.x.

training_pipeline.framework: TensorFlow (paper uses tf.gradients for autograd). Modern implementation can use PyTorch autograd.
training_pipeline.training_time: ~60 seconds for Burgers continuous (Titan X GPU)

burgers.exact_solution: Analytically available (referenced from Basdevant et al., 1986)

schrodinger.exact_solution: Simulated via Chebfun package, spectral Fourier (256 modes), RK4, dt=pi/2*1e-6

allen_cahn.exact_solution: Simulated via Chebfun package, spectral Fourier (512 modes), RK4, dt=1e-5

sensitivity_studies.burgers_continuous: Table 1 (Nu vs Nf), Table 2 (layers vs neurons)
sensitivity_studies.burgers_discrete: Table 3 (layers vs neurons), Table 4 (q vs dt)

baselines: Gaussian Process methods (Raissi et al. 2017, arXiv:1703.10230) — PINNs outperform by ~2 orders of magnitude in L2 error

implementation_assumptions.basis: Standard default for tanh networks in both TensorFlow and PyTorch

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Gauss-Legendre implicit RK tableau for discrete PINN
implementation_assumptions.basis: Paper states 'implicit Runge-Kutta' and references Iserles [24]; Gauss-Legendre is the canonical fully implicit A-stable family

implementation_assumptions.assumption: PyTorch + torch.autograd for automatic differentiation (modern reimplementation)
implementation_assumptions.basis: Paper uses TF1 tf.gradients; PyTorch autograd is the closest modern equivalent with same semantics

implementation_assumptions.assumption: L-BFGS via torch.optim.LBFGS with line search
implementation_assumptions.basis: Paper explicitly uses L-BFGS; PyTorch has a native implementation

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Collocation points sampled fresh every run via LHS (not fixed seed)
implementation_assumptions.basis: Paper states LHS sampling; no fixed seed mentioned
alternatives: Fixed seed for reproducibility (recommended in implementation)

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Exact Burgers solution generated via scipy or external solver for comparison
implementation_assumptions.basis: Paper says exact solution is analytically available; in practice a reference solver is needed

implementation_assumptions.assumption: No data normalization / input scaling described
implementation_assumptions.basis: Not mentioned; domains are already small (x in [-1,1] or [-5,5], t in [0,1])

ambiguities.description: Specific Gauss-Legendre RK tableau not printed in paper; referenced from [24]
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Standard q-stage Gauss-Legendre nodes computed via numpy/scipy
alternatives: Look up precomputed tableau from Butcher 1964

ambiguities.description: Whether h_x periodic BC is enforced at collocation points Nb or at all boundary evaluation points
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Nb randomly sampled time points on boundary (x=-5 and x=5 evaluated together)
alternatives: Fixed uniform grid on t for BC
